original_abstract: The capture and recycling of CO2 into value-added chemicals, such as alcohols, represents a vital strategy for mitigating greenhouse gas emissions. However, the inherent chemical inertness of CO2 remains a significant barrier, making its activation the rate-limiting step in efficient conversion processes. While bulk gold is catalytically inactive for hydrogenation, it can be activated when dispersed as nanoparticles across specific oxide supports. In this combined experimental and theoretical investigation, we demonstrate that electronic polarization at the metal-oxide interface—specifically within Au nanoparticles anchored on a CeOx/TiO2 substrate—creates potent active sites for CO2 adsorption. These interfacial sites facilitate low-pressure hydrogenation with enhanced selectivity toward methanol. Our findings highlight how tailoring localized electronic properties and interfacial structures can drive the development of high-performance catalysts for the selective transformation of CO2 into sustainable fuels.
humanized_abstract: CO2 capture and utilization to chemical commodities such as alcohols is believed to be one of the most efficient strategies to mitigate the release of green house gases. Due to its strong triple bond, the activation of CO2 remains the rate limiting step in catalytic CO2 conversion. Although the bulk gold is inactive to catalytic hydrogenation of CO2, it can be made catalytic when finely dispersed as nanogold particles on certain specific oxide supports. In this study, a synergetic experimental and theoretical investigation was performed to elucidate the catalytic activity of Au nanoparticles dispersed on a specific CeOx/TiO2 catalyst support. It is found that the activation of CO2 occurred at the Au-metal oxide interfacial site, which was induced by electronic polarization and thus served as an active site for CO2 adsorption, leading to high selectivity towards the formation of methanol at low pressure. This work therefore suggests the possibility of tailoring the catalyst activity at the molecular level by adjusting the localized electronic property and structure of the catalyst, which can facilitate the selective catalytic utilization of CO2 towards sustainable fuel production.

It is found that the activation of CO2 occurred at the Au-metal oxide interfacial site, which was induced by electronic polarization and thus served as an active site for CO2 adsorption, leading to high selectivity towards the formation of methanol at low pressure.
